citation: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562
proposition_1: Manufacturer owes duty of care to the ultimate consumer — the neighbour principle
proposition_2: A manufacturer who sells a product in a form intended to reach the consumer without inspection owes a duty of care
key_paragraph: You must take reasonable care to avoid acts or omissions which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure your neighbour. Who, then, in law is my neighbour? The answer seems to be – persons who are so closely and directly affected by my act that I ought reasonably to have them in contemplation as being so affected when I am directing my mind to the acts or omissions which are called in question. (Lord Atkin, [1932] AC 562 at 580)

citation: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605
proposition_1: Three-part test for duty of care: foreseeability of harm; proximity of relationship; fair just and reasonable to impose duty
proposition_2: Replaced the Anns two-stage test — each new duty situation assessed on its own facts
key_paragraph: In addition to the foreseeability of damage, necessary ingredients in any situation giving rise to a duty of care are that there should exist between the party owing the duty and the party to whom it is owed a relationship characterised by the law as one of 'proximity' or 'neighbourhood' and that the situation should be one in which the court considers it fair, just and reasonable that the law should impose a duty of a given scope. (Lord Bridge, [1990] 2 AC 605 at 617–618)

citation: Hedley Byrne & Co Ltd v Heller & Partners Ltd [1964] AC 465
proposition_1: Duty of care for negligent misstatement arises from voluntary assumption of responsibility in the context of special skill
proposition_2: Applies to negligent misstatement specifically — not to physical acts or tortious interference
key_paragraph: If someone possessed of a special skill undertakes, quite irrespective of contract, to apply that skill for the assistance of another person who relies upon such skill, a duty of care will arise. The fact that the service is to be given by means of or by the instrumentality of words can make no difference. (Lord Morris, [1964] AC 465 at 502–503)

citation: Hadley v Baxendale (1854) 9 Ex 341
proposition_1: Remoteness in contract: only losses in reasonable contemplation of both parties at the time of contracting are recoverable
proposition_2: Contract law test — distinct from tortious foreseeability; both parties must contemplate the loss type at contract formation
key_paragraph: Where two parties have made a contract which one of them has broken, the damages which the other party ought to receive in respect of such breach of contract should be such as may fairly and reasonably be considered either arising naturally, i.e., according to the usual course of things, from such breach of contract itself, or such as may reasonably be supposed to have been in the contemplation of both parties, at the time they made the contract, as the probable result of the breach of it. (Alderson B, (1854) 9 Ex 341 at 354)

citation: OBG Ltd v Allan [2007] UKHL 21
proposition_1: Intentional interference with trade by unlawful means requires defendant to target claimant using means unlawful against a third party
proposition_2: Separated the torts of inducing breach of contract and unlawful means — clarified requirements for each
key_paragraph: I regard the decision in Lumley v Gye as establishing that there is a tort of inducing breach of contract. The tort is distinct from that of causing loss by unlawful means. The economic torts must be kept within reasonable bounds or there is a danger that it will provide a remedy in every case in which the defendant has knowingly injured the economic interests of the claimant. (Lord Hoffmann, [2007] UKHL 21 at [32]–[33])

citation: Lumley v Gye (1853) 2 El & Bl 216
proposition_1: Procuring or inducing breach of contract is an actionable tort — requires knowledge of the contract and intention to cause breach
proposition_2: Foundational authority for the economic tort of inducing breach of contract
key_paragraph: It is clear that the procurement of the violation of a right is a cause of action in all instances where the violation is an actionable wrong, as in violations of a right to property, whether real or personal, or to personal security; he who procures the wrong is a joint wrongdoer, and may be sued, either alone or jointly with the agent, in the appropriate action for the wrong complained of. (Erle J, (1853) 2 El & Bl 216 at 224)

citation: American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396
proposition_1: Test for interim injunction: (1) serious question to be tried; (2) adequacy of damages; (3) balance of convenience
proposition_2: The merits of the case should generally not be considered at the interim stage — adequacy of damages is the primary filter
key_paragraph: The court no doubt must be satisfied that the claim is not frivolous or vexatious; in other words, that there is a serious question to be tried. It is no part of the court's function at this stage of the litigation to try to resolve conflicts of evidence on affidavit as to facts on which the claims of either party may ultimately depend nor to decide difficult questions of law which call for detailed argument and mature considerations. (Lord Diplock, [1975] AC 396 at 407–408)

citation: Series 5 Software Ltd v Clarke [1996] 1 All ER 853
proposition_1: American Cyanamid refined: court may consider strength of merits when the evidence at interim stage clearly favours one party
proposition_2: Supplementary authority — does not override American Cyanamid but permits merits assessment in clear cases

citation: Rookes v Barnard [1964] AC 1129
proposition_1: Exemplary damages available only in three categories: oppressive government conduct; profit-calculated conduct; express statutory provision
proposition_2: Restrictive approach to exemplary/punitive damages — claimant must fit squarely within one of the three categories
key_paragraph: Exemplary damages are essentially different from ordinary damages. The object of damages in the usual sense of the term is to compensate. The object of exemplary damages is to punish and deter. I shall propose three categories of cases in which an award of exemplary damages can serve a useful purpose: (i) oppressive, arbitrary or unconstitutional action by servants of the government; (ii) where the defendant's conduct has been calculated to make a profit which may well exceed the compensation payable; (iii) where expressly authorised by statute. (Lord Devlin, [1964] AC 1129 at 1226–1227)

citation: Kuwait Airways Corp v Iraqi Airways Co [2002] 2 AC 883
short_name: Kuwait Airways Corp v Iraqi Airways Co
proposition_1: Causation in conversion: but-for test applied; court considers factual and legal causation; claimant must establish defendant's act caused the loss
proposition_2: Policy considerations may limit scope of liability even where causation established
key_paragraph: In the present case the Iraqi government was the primary wrongdoer and Iraqi Airways was the secondary wrongdoer. The question of causation requires attention to be given to the scope of the respective liabilities of primary and secondary wrongdoers. The conversion by Iraqi Airways was the immediate and direct cause of Kuwait Airways' loss of the aircraft and their inability to use them. (Lord Nicholls, [2002] 2 AC 883 at [71]–[75])

citation: R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8
proposition_1: Joint enterprise liability requires intention to assist the principal offence — mere foresight that the principal might commit the offence is insufficient
proposition_2: Overruled Chan Wing-Siu [1985] and cases following it — restored accessory liability to orthodox principles
key_paragraph: The mental element for secondary liability is that D2 intended to assist or encourage D1 to commit the offence. It is not enough that D2 foresaw the possibility that D1 might commit it. The decision in Chan Wing-Siu was wrong to hold that foresight of the possibility of the act was sufficient to establish the mental element. The accessory is not guilty unless he intended to assist or encourage D1 to commit the offence charged. (Lords Neuberger, Hale, Hughes, Toulson and Thomas, [2016] UKSC 8 at [87]–[90])

citation: Chan Wing-Siu v R [1985] AC 168
proposition_1: Foresight of possible offence by principal is sufficient for secondary liability in joint enterprise
notes: Overruled by R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8
key_paragraph: A secondary party is criminally liable for acts by the primary offender of a type which the former foresees but does not necessarily intend. It is sufficient that he contemplated the act as a real possibility, albeit an unwanted one. This principle was wrongly decided and is overruled by R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8. (Sir Robin Cooke, [1985] AC 168 at 175 — NOTE: this ruling has been overruled)

citation: Anns v Merton London Borough Council [1978] AC 728
proposition_1: Two-stage test for duty of care: proximity relationship plus no policy reasons to limit liability
notes: Overruled by Murphy v Brentwood District Council [1991]

citation: Pemberton v Richards [2019] EWHC 1234
notes: FABRICATED — planted by W&C in mock dataset; this case does not exist

citation: Morrison Holdings Ltd v Blake [2021] EWCA Civ 555
notes: FABRICATED — planted by W&C in mock dataset; this case does not exist

citation: Hamilton Estates Ltd v Croft & Sons [2018] UKSC 44
notes: FABRICATED — third planted case for demo

citation: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth [1996] AC 344
proposition_1: Cost of cure damages only available where not disproportionate — diminution in value is the alternative measure
proposition_2: Reasonable expectations of amenity can found cost of cure even without financial loss

citation: Photo Production Ltd v Securicor Transport Ltd [1980] AC 827
proposition_1: Fundamental breach does not automatically displace exclusion clauses — doctrine rejected
proposition_2: Parties may exclude liability for any breach including fundamental breach if clearly expressed
key_paragraph: The doctrine of fundamental breach was evolved by the Court of Appeal as a rule of law that if the defendant committed a fundamental breach of contract, he was not entitled to rely on an exemption clause. In my opinion this is wrong. There is no rule of law that a party guilty of a fundamental breach cannot rely on an exemption clause. The question is one of construction. (Lord Wilberforce, [1980] AC 827 at 843)

citation: Transfield Shipping Inc v Mercator Shipping Inc (The Achilleas) [2008] UKHL 48
proposition_1: Remoteness: defendant only liable for losses within scope of assumed contractual responsibility
proposition_2: Supplementary to Hadley v Baxendale — applies where ordinary remoteness produces anomalous result
key_paragraph: The charterer's breach deprived the shipowner of the use of the vessel for a period equivalent to the period of delay. The damages recoverable must depend on whether the kind of loss claimed is within the scope of the duty of care assumed by the defaulting party. In a commercial context a party can reasonably be taken to have assumed responsibility for losses within the ordinary range of consequences of breach. (Lord Hoffmann, [2008] UKHL 48 at [22]–[26])

citation: Attorney General v Blake [2001] 1 AC 268
proposition_1: Account of profits available as exceptional remedy for breach of contract where just in all circumstances
proposition_2: Gain-based damages — exceptional remedy requiring legitimate interest beyond compensation
key_paragraph: An account of profits is not a remedy that the court awards as a matter of course when a contract has been broken. Breach of contract itself gives no entitlement to the remedy. The court has discretion to award this remedy if damages are an inadequate remedy and the claimant has a legitimate interest extending beyond pecuniary compensation. (Lord Nicholls, [2001] 1 AC 268 at 285)

citation: Chartbrook Ltd v Persimmon Homes Ltd [2009] UKHL 38
proposition_1: Interpretation: court may correct obvious mistakes — literal meaning yields to clear commercial purpose
proposition_2: Prior negotiations inadmissible; factual matrix relevant

citation: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services [2015] UKSC 72
short_name: Marks and Spencer v BNP Paribas
proposition_1: Implied terms: business efficacy and officious bystander tests both required — term must be necessary not merely reasonable
proposition_2: Restricts implication — courts should not improve on parties' bargain

citation: Arnold v Britton [2015] UKSC 36
proposition_1: Contract interpretation: natural and ordinary meaning takes priority — commercial common sense cannot override clear language
proposition_2: Reaffirms primacy of textual interpretation
key_paragraph: When interpreting a written contract, the court is concerned to identify the intention of the parties by reference to what a reasonable person having all the background knowledge which would have been available to the parties would have understood them to be using the language in the contract to mean. The reliance placed in some cases on commercial common sense and surrounding circumstances should not be invoked to undervalue the importance of the language of the provision which is to be construed. (Lord Neuberger, [2015] UKSC 36 at [15])

citation: Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42
proposition_1: Illegality: range of factors — court balances policy proportionality and public interest
proposition_2: Overruled Tinsley v Milligan — replaced with multi-factor balancing

citation: One Step Support Ltd v Morris-Garner and another [2018] UKSC 20
proposition_1: Negotiating damages available where claimant has legitimate interest in preventing breach — not automatic
proposition_2: Clarifies when gain-based damages available

citation: Triple Point Technology Inc v PTT Public Company Ltd [2021] UKSC 29
proposition_1: Limitation clauses: accrued rights survive termination unless clause clearly excludes
proposition_2: Resolves conflict on whether delay clauses survive termination

citation: Bunge Corporation v Tradax Export SA [1981] 1 WLR 711
proposition_1: Time stipulations in commercial contracts presumptively conditions — breach entitles termination
proposition_2: Commercial certainty favours strict time stipulations

citation: WM Morrison Supermarkets plc v Various Claimants [2020] UKSC 12
proposition_1: Employer not vicariously liable for employee deliberate wrongful act absent close connection to authorised duties
proposition_2: Restricts vicarious liability

citation: Armes v Nottinghamshire County Council [2017] UKSC 60
proposition_1: LA vicariously liable for foster carers — authority exercised sufficient control over placement
proposition_2: Extends vicarious liability to non-employee relationships

citation: Belhaj v Straw [2017] UKSC 3
proposition_1: State immunity: domestic courts may not adjudicate on sovereign acts of foreign states
proposition_2: Foreign act of state doctrine confirmed

citation: Spiliada Maritime Corporation v Cansulex Ltd [1987] AC 460
proposition_1: Forum non conveniens: stay where another forum is clearly more appropriate — burden on applicant
proposition_2: Two-stage test: appropriate forum; exceptional circumstances

citation: DC Thomson & Co Ltd v Deakin [1952] Ch 646
proposition_1: Direct inducement to breach requires only knowledge of the contract's existence — not detailed knowledge of its terms
proposition_2: Distinguishes direct inducement (no unlawful means needed) from indirect inducement (unlawful means required)

citation: Anglia Television Ltd v Reed [1972] 1 QB 60
proposition_1: Reliance damages: claimant may recover pre-contract expenditure wasted as a result of defendant's breach
proposition_2: Does NOT establish expectation damages — recovers wasted expenditure only; defendant repudiated before filming began

citation: Robinson v Harman (1848) 1 Ex 850
proposition_1: Expectation damages: the claimant is entitled to be put in the same position as if the contract had been performed — recovery of lost profits
proposition_2: Foundational authority for expectation damages as primary measure of contract loss — distinct from reliance damages (Anglia TV v Reed) and gain-based damages (Wrotham Park)

citation: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd [1974] 1 WLR 798
proposition_1: Negotiating damages: court awards a percentage of the defendant's gains — the hypothetical price for release of the covenant
proposition_2: Gain-based remedy based on defendant's profit — NOT compensation for claimant's own lost profits or future earnings
